findings.quote: Building a clock that kept accurate time under conditions at sea.
findings.note: The Harrison marine-chronometer story makes B the known obstacle. Distractor D (lunar-distance method) is a genuine competing longitude technique, so options are substantive; world-knowledge answerability, not a form leak.

findings.quote: The field was quick to ask whether profiting from distraction serves users.
findings.note: D can be eliminated blind as world-knowledge-false (attention-economy critiques hold the field was slow to self-question); A and B are both plausibly supported, so eliminations are materially better than chance without being decisive.

findings.quote: Which of the following statements is supported by the passage?
findings.note: Question style: stem phrasing unattested in the ELF corpus (0/405 prompts use 'supported by'; nearest is a single 'What is true according to the opening paragraph?') and it carries no content anchor, where every exemplar stem names its topic or scope — contrast var-2026-verb1-ELF-040 ('...public opinion on crime in 18th century Britain?') and host-2023-verb1-ELF-040 ('What can be concluded from the text about El Greco?'). Reads as generic test-bank register, not HP.

findings.quote: Companies in the attention economy compete for the hours a person is awake.
findings.note: Question style: options A and B recycle distinctive verbatim content-word runs from the passage ('the hours a person is awake'; 'psychology of the slot machine'), where exemplar options are paraphrases — contrast var-2026-verb1-ELF-040's key 'Criminals' behaviour is unlikely to change for the better' vs the passage's 'spiral down into a life of villainy'. Surface word-match at this density sits outside the exemplar option register.

findings.quote: The phrase "attention economy" was coined long before the smartphone
findings.note: Genre/length: passage (~190 words) runs above the ~100-160-word single-short-text band the exemplars occupy (136/159/136 words); band conformance is M-BANDS' call, noted here only as edge-of-range.

findings.quote: Factors other than wolves may explain much of the recovery.
findings.note: Recent Yellowstone re-analyses caveat the trophic cascade exactly as A; B and C are world-knowledge-false (wolves had effect; elk decreased). Distractors are substantive claims about the same system, so world-knowledge answerability routed to adjudication.

findings.quote: On a conference in 2014, several researchers presented informations that questioned how much of the recovery was really the wolves' doing
findings.note: register: 'On a conference' and uncountable-mass 'informations' are non-native English errors that a real New Scientist/Economist editor would never let through — betrays synthetic/ESL authorship despite otherwise magazine-perfect prose

findings.quote: It blocks the chemicals that produce sleepiness.
findings.note: The adenosine-blocking mechanism is well-known science, favouring A. C and D are origin/legend facts commonly present in such passages (substantive distractors, not remote filler), so a passage would still ground the mechanism choice.

findings.quote: Let's be honest — you probably can't function without your morning coffee, and you're definitely not alone! ... Ready to have your mind blown?
findings.note: class 4: register break — clickbait-listicle cadence with contractions, exclamation hype and direct reader address, wholly out of keeping with edited academic-magazine ELF register. Grammatical native English, so not lethal; convicts as a register failure. Editor's rewrite: rebuild in expository third person, e.g. 'Coffee is among the world's most popular drinks, yet much about it is little known.'

findings.quote: And here's the kicker — that "coffee" buzz isn't just in your head. ... How cool is that?
findings.note: class 4: marketing/informal cadence ('here's the kicker', 'How cool is that?') breaks the serious-magazine register expected of ELF sources.

findings.quote: Ready to have your mind blown? Here are five amazing facts that will make you love your daily cup even more.
findings.note: class 4: clickbait-listicle register, wholly incompatible with the serious-magazine expository tone of authentic ELF. Direct reader address plus hype cadence. Grammatically native, so not lethal, but an editor at an academic-magazine would strike the register entirely.

findings.quote: And here's the kicker — that "coffee" buzz isn't just in your head.
findings.note: class 4: contractions and colloquial framing ('here's the kicker', 'Pretty wild, huh?') break the expository register expected of the section.

dissenting_language_vote.quote: Ready to have your mind blown? Here are five amazing facts that will make you love your daily cup even more.
dissenting_language_vote.note: class 4: wholesale register failure — clickbait listicle with direct reader address, nothing like the serious-magazine expository prose of authentic ELF; no rewrite salvages the genre.
class 4: sudden informality and chatty interjection; a professional editor at an Economist/SciAm-register publication strikes the entire mode, not the sentence.

dissenting_language_vote.quote: So next time you grab a cup, remember: you're basically drinking science. How cool is that?
dissenting_language_vote.note: class 4: marketing cadence plus contraction-heavy direct address; incompatible with formal exposition.

findings.quote: The work of women appointed during the war showed that the arguments were flawed.
findings.note: C ('completely changed') and D ('best of all') are overstated absolutes and B is tangential (national unity); the measured historically-accurate reading A can be leaned to blind, materially better than chance but not decided.

findings.quote: Virtually nothing of the Pantheon erected by Agrippa remains today.
findings.note: World knowledge of the Pantheon (current building is Hadrian's rebuild; little of Agrippa's original survives) lets one lean A and eliminate B, but the exact key is not decided blind; substantive distractors, genuine comprehension.

findings.quote: delprov elf engelsk läsförståelse what is the main
findings.note: 8-gram containment 0.0115 exceeds flag threshold 0.01 (3/260 grams shared)

findings.quote: The basic idea of Silent Spring remains controversial.
findings.note: Domain knowledge (industry never fully accepted Carson; her core idea stayed contested) leans C and marks B false; distractors are substantive positions on the book's legacy, so world-knowledge answerability, not a form leak.

findings.quote: Många arter är känsliga för föroreningar som svaveldioxid.
findings.note: Domain knowledge that lichens are classic SO2-sensitive bioindicators points to A; but the distractors (fast growth, lichenometry dating, competition in clean air) are substantive real facts, not filler, so a passage would still ground the choice.

findings.quote: De markerade gränsen mot det farliga och okända.
findings.note: Van Duzer's well-known thesis is that map monsters marked the dangerous/unknown, so domain knowledge picks A; distractors (purely decorative, real-animal sightings, illiteracy) are substantive competing readings, so this is genuine comprehension routed to adjudication.

findings.quote: Okej, så här är grejen med gamla kartor: de är egentligen ganska sjukt opålitliga.
findings.note: Class 5 register break: the entire passage is talshow-/blogg-Swedish ('sjukt', 'grejen', direct 'du'-address) where the exam demands edited sakprosa. Idiomatically native but the wrong register for a LÄS passage.

findings.quote: som har kollat på jättemånga sådana kartor
findings.note: Class 5 register break: colloquialisms 'kollat på' and 'jättemånga' (both explicitly off-register markers) in what should be edited essay prose.

findings.quote: att kartan och verkligheten inte alltid är polare. Kanske borde vi återinföra monstren, typ som en påminnelse
findings.note: Class 5 register break: slang 'polare' and filler 'typ' break the sakprosa register expected of exam texts.

findings.quote: Att tystnaden är en historiskt skapad norm snarare än något givet.
findings.note: C and D are absurd absolutes (moderns banned all talk; reading aloud signalled poor education) and can be eliminated blind; between A and B the 'main idea' of an expository text is the reframing A rather than the naive B, so 2+ options fall passage-blind though not decisively.

findings.quote: Att gemensam förvaltning kan fungera hållbart med rätt institutioner.
findings.note: This is Ostrom's Nobel-winning thesis against Hardin; anyone who knows Ostrom picks A. But B and D are opposite scholarly positions (private ownership superior / only the state can manage) and are substantively plausible stances, so this is world-knowledge answerability, not a structural leak.

findings.quote: Uppfunna traditioner uppstår ofta i tider av snabb förändring.
findings.note: Hobsbawm's 'Invention of Tradition' thesis is exactly A; domain knowledge also flags C as false (tartan systematised 18th-19th c., not medieval). Distractors are substantive positions/misreadings, so world-knowledge answerability rather than form leak.

findings.quote: en obruten highlandtradition
findings.note: class 5/6: raw English 'highland' fused into a lowercase Swedish compound; edited sakprosa would keep the register Swedish. native alternative: 'en obruten hoglandstradition' / 'en obruten tradition i (skotska) hoglanderna'.

findings.quote: Den skotska klantartanen
findings.note: class 1: 'klantartan' (klan+tartan) is a coined compound that also invites a 'klant-' (klumpig) misparse; a Swedish editor would more likely write 'det skotska klanmonstret' or 'den skotska tartanen'. native alternative: 'det skotska klanmonstret'.

findings.quote: en obruten highlandtradition
findings.note: class 6: 'highlandtradition' reads as an anglicism where idiomatic sakprosa uses a Swedish head; a native copy editor pauses here. native alternative: 'en obruten hoglandstradition'.

findings.quote: textilfabrikanter som såg en marknad
findings.note: class 2: 'sag en marknad' is a clipped calque of English 'saw a market'; idiomatic Swedish completes the object or uses a different verb. native alternative: 'som sag en marknad for monstren' / 'som vadrade en affarsmojlighet'.

findings.quote: en obruten highlandtradition
findings.note: class 6: on re-read, borderline - Swedish press does borrow 'Highland' in Scottish contexts - but the lowercase native compound 'highlandtradition' still reads off where 'hoglandstradition' is idiomatic; recorded as taste-level. native alternative: 'en obruten hoglandstradition'.

findings.quote: Den skotska klantartanen
findings.note: class 1: coined compound klan+tartan; understandable but a Swedish editor would likely prefer 'det skotska klanmonstret'. native alternative: 'det skotska klanmonstret'.

findings.quote: På grund av dåtidens tillverkningsmetod.
findings.note: The glass-flow / glass-is-a-liquid claims (B, C) are the famous debunked myth, so a knowledgeable solver picks the manufacturing-method key A. Comprehension of the debunking still requires the passage; distractors are substantive misconceptions, not remote filler.

findings.quote: Att det sker en tillvänjningsprocess som förskjuter tidigare uppfattningar om rätt och fel.
findings.note: C paraphrases the standard meaning of the idiom 'sluttande plan' (gradual habituation shifting norms), so a reader who knows the phrase leans C; other options name specific consequences and remain substantive, so a passage would still be needed to confirm Norlen's usage.

findings.quote: Etniskt splittrade samhällen.
findings.note: Domain analysis of the Arab-Spring literature cites ethnic fragmentation as an obstacle to a coordinated uprising, favouring A. From form alone the four options are parallel single noun-phrases with no cluster asymmetry, so any pick is domain reasoning, not a structural tell.
